items.title: NHIS for foreigners: when it's automatic and when you have to enrol yourself
items.summary: If you've been in Korea for six months you're already in the National Health Insurance Service, ready or not. The question is whether your employer or school is doing it for you.
items.body:
Korean health insurance (NHIS) is mandatory for almost every foreigner staying past the six-month mark. The detail people miss is that it's not opt-in — it just *starts*, and you owe the bill from that month.

## Who enrols you

- **Full-time employees** — your company handles it. Look at your payslip; the deduction shows up as 건강보험.
- **University students (D-4 / D-2)** — your school usually arranges a group enrolment. Ask the international office. If they say nothing, *you* are responsible.
- **Self-employed, freelancers, dependants of citizens** — you enrol at your local NHIS branch within 90 days of crossing the six-month line.

## What it costs

Monthly contribution is around KRW 70,000–150,000 for students paying solo, scaling with income for employees. It's cheaper than almost any private alternative and includes hospital visits, dental basics, and emergency care.

## Where it falls short

NHIS covers a lot but not everything. Cosmetic procedures, premium private rooms, and most non-Korean prescriptions are out-of-pocket. Foreign travel insurance is still worth keeping for the first six months when you aren't covered yet.

## What to do tomorrow

Call **1577-1000** (English available) or use the NHIS English portal in the related links below. Confirm whether you've already been auto-enrolled. If you're a student, ask your school whether the group enrolment includes you.

items.title: Finding an English-speaking doctor in Seoul: three places that actually work
items.summary: Most large Korean hospitals have a foreign patient centre. Knowing which to use for what saves a lot of waiting.
items.body:
When something goes wrong with your body in Korea, the worst time to start Googling is the moment it hurts. Bookmark these in advance.

## For real emergencies

**E-Gen (e-gen.or.kr)** is the national emergency medical portal. The English map shows the nearest open emergency rooms and 24-hour pharmacies. If you can't open the site, dial **119** — the call centre connects you to an English interpreter within 30 seconds.

For anything less than 'I need an ambulance,' dial **1339** instead. It's the medical advice line and they can route you to a nearby clinic that handles your specific issue.

## For scheduled appointments

The big three for English-speaking care in Seoul:

- **Asan Medical Center International Health Service** — strongest for general medicine, oncology, cardiology.
- **Severance Hospital International Health Care Center** — strong on women's health, paediatrics.
- **Samsung Medical Center Global Health Care Center** — best workflow for fast check-ups, billing is the cleanest for insurance claims.

All three publish English booking forms; for first visits, calling is usually faster than the web form.

## For everyday stuff in Itaewon / Hannam

Several expat-oriented clinics around Itaewon take walk-ins — Yonsei Bom Hospital, International Clinic. They are not cheaper than a regular Korean clinic, but the English fluency is reliable.

## The unsexy tip

Keep a photo on your phone of your passport + ARC + the Korean name of any medication you take. Half the friction at the front desk evaporates.

items.title: Buying K-pop concert tickets from overseas: how it really works in 2026
items.summary: Interpark and Melon are the two real options. Either way, the difficulty is not the queue — it's identity verification.
items.body:
Two platforms run almost every major Korean concert: **Interpark** and **Melon Ticket**. Both have English flows, both occasionally fail on foreign cards. Here's what reliably works.

## Step 1 — Make the account before you need it

Don't wait until ticket day. Both sites require an email-verified account and Interpark's global checkout sometimes asks for a passport number. Set this up days in advance and you skip the panic.

## Step 2 — Use a card that knows you're abroad

Foreign cards work, but Korean 3-D Secure systems sometimes block them. The two that fail least often in my experience:

- Visa / Mastercard issued by a major US or EU bank, with online purchases enabled.
- A Wise multi-currency card pre-loaded with KRW.

Kakao Pay and Naver Pay are only available if you have a Korean phone number.

## Step 3 — Know when fan-club pre-sale beats general

Most large groups release tickets in tiers: official global fan club, then domestic fan club, then general public. The global tier is usually 20–40% of the inventory and it's released a few days before general. If you're flying in for one specific group, joining the global fan club ahead of time pays for itself in one show.

## When it goes wrong

If payment fails after seat selection, you have about 5 minutes before the seat releases. Refresh, try a different card, or move to the next-best seat in the same section. Do not refresh the browser — open a new tab and reselect from your account page.

items.title: 18 hours at Incheon: a layover plan that's actually worth doing
items.summary: If your transit window is longer than 6 hours and your passport qualifies for visa-free entry, leaving the airport is usually the better call.
items.body:
Incheon is one of the few major airports where 'just stay at the airport' is a respectable option — the sleep zones, showers and food are all genuinely good. But once you cross 12 hours, the city beats the lounge.

## Quick rules

- Most Western and Northeast Asian passports get visa-free entry of 30–90 days. K-ETA still required for boarding the inbound flight.
- Bags can sit in airport storage (Terminal 1 Level B1) for ~KRW 6,000 per piece per day.
- AREX Express to Seoul Station: **43 minutes, KRW 11,000**. Cheapest fast way in.

## A 18-hour plan that doesn't kill you

Assume you land at 06:00 and fly out at 24:00.

1. **06:30 – 07:30** — Land, immigration, breakfast at the food court (Korean Air's first-floor 김밥 is reliable).
2. **07:30 – 08:15** — Bag drop at storage. Buy a T-money card at any convenience store, top up KRW 10,000.
3. **08:30** — AREX Express to Seoul Station.
4. **09:30 – 12:00** — Walk the **Gyeongbokgung → Bukchon → Insadong** loop. Tea house, a museum if you want, lunch in Insadong.
5. **12:30 – 14:30** — Subway to Hongdae or Gangnam for cafés and people-watching.
6. **15:00** — Subway back to Seoul Station, AREX back to ICN.
7. **16:30** — Through security, dinner in the terminal (the basement food court beats the upper floors).
8. **18:00 – 23:00** — Lounge, shower, sleep zone (look for the 'Rest & Relax' signs near gates 40 and 121).

## The unromantic warning

Leave at least **4 hours** between getting back to ICN and boarding. Immigration and security can be slow on heavy days. Do not cut this close.

items.title: Halal food in Seoul: a real list, not a 'top 10' list
items.summary: Outside of Itaewon's Islamic Street, finding certified halal in Seoul takes more work than it should. Here's where it's reliable.
items.body:
Seoul is friendlier for Muslim travellers than it was even five years ago, but most restaurants still aren't certified. The good news: enough have been audited that you can plan multiple days without repeating yourself.

## What 'halal' means here

Korea Tourism Organization tags restaurants in four levels: **Self Certified, Muslim Friendly, Halal Certified, Pork Free**. Only the second two are reliably halal for strict observers. The KTO maintains an English directory with these labels.

## Reliable in central Seoul

- **Yongsan / Itaewon Islamic Street** — anchored by the **Seoul Central Mosque**. Eid, Salam, Kervan all have official certification. Walking distance from Itaewon station exit 3.
- **Murree Pakistani Restaurant** (Itaewon) — Pakistan-style, halal certified, generous portions.
- **Plant Cafe** (multiple locations) — entirely vegan, so de-facto safe.
- **Sultan Kebab** (Hongdae) — Turkish, halal certified.

## Outside Seoul

Busan's Haeundae area has a small but solid set. Jeju's tourist zones increasingly carry KTO 'Muslim Friendly' tags. Outside major tourist cities it gets thin.

## At the airport

Incheon Airport Terminal 1 has a prayer room near gate 25 and Terminal 2 near gate 252. A few food stalls — Mr. Pizza Halal and a Turkish-style outlet — carry certifications visible on the menu.

## When the menu is silent

If nothing is certified and you're hungry, vegetarian Korean temple food (사찰음식) is the safer fallback than 'just no pork'. Sanchon in Insadong is the best-known temple-food restaurant in Seoul.

items.title: K-ETA: what visa-free visitors actually need to do before flying
items.summary: If your passport gives you visa-free entry to Korea, K-ETA is the one form you cannot forget. A quick rundown of timing, cost, and the bits people miss.
items.body:
Most travellers who can enter Korea visa-free still need a K-ETA — the electronic travel authorisation — and it has to be approved before you board your flight.

## The basics

- **When to apply:** at least 72 hours before departure.
- **Cost:** roughly KRW 10,000 per application.
- **Validity:** 3 years (you can reuse it for multiple trips).
- **Where:** the official portal only — see the link below.

## What people miss

1. **Photo rules.** Plain background, full face, no glasses. Most rejections are bad photos.
2. **Passport scan quality.** The corners must be visible. Don't crop.
3. **Email confirmation.** If it doesn't arrive within 24 hours, check spam. The QR code in the email is what airlines will scan.

## If you get refused

A K-ETA refusal does not mean you cannot enter Korea — it just means you need a regular visa instead. Apply through the nearest Korean consulate.

## Where to apply

The only official portal is **k-eta.go.kr**. Avoid third-party 'application services' that charge extra.

items.title: T-money: the one thing to buy in your first hour in Seoul
items.summary: A small rechargeable card that runs every subway, bus and most taxis in Korea. Sold at any convenience store for KRW 4,000.
items.body:
Public transport in Korea runs on a single rechargeable card called **T-money**. You can technically pay in cash, but you'll get worse fares and slower boarding.

## Where to get one

- Any convenience store inside or near Incheon Airport (GS25, CU, 7-Eleven, eMart24).
- Subway station vending machines, but the in-store option is faster on day one.
- The plain card costs KRW 4,000. Designer 'Cashbee' or character cards work the same way.

## How to use it

1. **Top up** at any convenience store or subway machine. KRW 10,000 is enough for a half-day of riding.
2. **Tap on** when entering the subway / bus.
3. **Tap off** when exiting the subway, or when leaving the bus through the back door — this is important, you'll be charged extra if you forget.

## Where it works

- All subway lines nationwide.
- All city and intercity buses (with the back-door tap rule).
- Most taxis (a small ‘T-money’ sticker on the dashboard means yes).
- Convenience stores accept it as a payment method too.

## Refunds

Return the card at any larger subway station's customer service desk and they'll refund your remaining balance minus a small fee.

items.title: Extending a D-4 student visa: the steps, the paperwork, the calls
items.summary: If you've been on a D-4 for a year or more, you've probably had to renew it. Here's the actual flow most students go through.
items.body:
Extending a D-4 visa is more paperwork than scary, but the order matters. The wrong order costs you a wasted trip to the immigration office.

## Before you go

- **Book a slot** on Hi Korea at least two weeks before your visa expires. Walk-ins are not allowed at most offices.
- **Pull a tuition receipt** from your school's international office — they know which forms.
- **Save a screenshot** of your bank account showing at least KRW 9 million (rough requirement, varies). A statement covering the last 3 months is safer.

## What to bring on the day

1. Passport + ARC.
2. Application form (download from Hi Korea, fill in advance).
3. Tuition receipt + admission certificate.
4. Bank statement (printed).
5. Proof of address (rental contract or dormitory letter).
6. Color photo (the immigration office machine works too).

## At the office

Take a number, give your documents, pay the fee (KRW 60,000 for extension). They keep your ARC and post the new one to your address in 2–3 weeks. You can use your passport as ID in the meantime, but you can't leave the country.

## When in doubt

Call **1345** — the immigration call centre supports 20 languages. They will not file the application for you, but they will tell you exactly which document you are missing for your case.
